snp2.5:1.2: Thus have I heard. On one occasion the Blessed One was dwelling at Gayā at the Ṭaṃkita Bed, the abode of the yakkha Sūciloma. Now on that [48] occasion the yakkha Khara and the yakkha Sūciloma passed by not far from the Blessed One. Then the yakkha Khara said to the yakkha Sūciloma: “He is an ascetic.”
snp2.5:1.3: “He is not an ascetic; he is a sham ascetic. I’ll soon find out whether he is an ascetic or a sham ascetic.”
snp2.5:1.4: Then the yakkha Sūciloma approached the Blessed One. Having approached, he leaned toward the Blessed One. The Blessed One drew back. Then the yakkha Sūciloma said to the Blessed One: “Are you afraid of me, ascetic?”
snp2.5:1.5: “I’m not afraid of you, friend. It is just that your touch is bad.”
snp2.5:1.6: “I’ll ask you a question, ascetic. If you won’t answer me, I’ll drive you insane or split your heart or grab you by the feet and hurl you across the Ganges.”
snp2.5:1.7: “I do not see anyone in this world, friend, with its devas, Māra, and Brahmā, in this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans, who could drive me insane or split my heart or grab me by the feet and hurl me across the Ganges. But ask whatever you want, friend.”
snp2.5:2.2: The yakkha Sūciloma then addressed the Blessed One in verse:
snp2.5:2.3:
270
“What is the source of lust and hatred?
Where are born discontent, delight, and hair-raising terror?
Having originated from what do the mind’s thoughts
toss one around as boys toss up a crow?”
(1)
snp2.5:2.4:
271
“Lust and hatred have their source here;
from this are born discontent, delight, and hair-raising terror.
Having originated from this, the mind’s thoughts
toss one around as boys toss up a crow. (2) [49]
snp2.5:2.5:
272
“Born from affection, arisen from oneself,
like the trunk-born shoots of the banyan tree,
manifold, attached to sensual pleasures,
like a māluvā
creeper stretched across the woods. (3)
snp2.5:2.6:
273
“Those who understand their source,
they dispel it — listen, O yakkha! —
they cross this flood so hard to cross,
uncrossed before, for no renewed existence.” (4)
